title: The Psychology Behind Why Some Homes Feel Good But Most Donãt: Interior Design Principles Explained
description:
in Architecture, Life |   	April 29th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Though it may have enjoyed occaôÙsionôÙal waves of pop-culôÙturôÙal presôÙtige ove
body:
in Architecture, Life |   	April 29th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Though it may have enjoyed occaôÙsionôÙal waves of pop-culôÙturôÙal presôÙtige over the years, inteôÙriôÙor design remains an overôÙlooked art. That is to say, few bothôÙer to appreôÙciôÙate, or even to notice, its simôÙiôÙlarôÙiôÙties with othôÙer, more ãseriôÙousã forms of human endeavôÙor. Watch the recent Five by Nine video above,ô and even if youãve felt reaôÙsonôÙably conôÙtent with wherôÙevôÙer your own couch, chairs, and tables have come to rest up until now, youãll soon find yourôÙself conôÙsidôÙerôÙing which prinôÙciôÙples of inteôÙriôÙor design youãve always been unknowôÙingôÙly vioôÙlatôÙing. For our eyes ãreadã a room just as it would a paraôÙgraph, or even a paintôÙing, and they sense instincôÙtiveôÙly if someôÙthingãs wrong ã or, worse, if too much is right.
One comôÙmon amaôÙteur misôÙtake is to arrange rooms so thatô ãeveryôÙthing lives on one sinôÙgle horôÙiôÙzonôÙtal band that starts at the floor and ends around two and a half feet up.ã With all the furôÙniôÙture on more or less a sinôÙgle levôÙel, your eye ãhas no reaôÙson to travôÙel upward or into the corôÙners,ã and thus perôÙceives a strangeôÙly flatôÙtened space.
ãPlacôÙing visuôÙal interôÙest at varyôÙing altiôÙtudesã creôÙates a more comôÙplex visuôÙal path, which conôÙvinces the brain itãs in a more expanôÙsive (or indeed expenôÙsive) space. MountôÙing curôÙtain rods well above the winôÙdow frame also goes a long way toward creôÙatôÙing this same overôÙall effect. The use of verôÙtiôÙcal lines in genôÙerôÙal,ô in the form of bookôÙcasôÙes, wall texôÙtures, or anyôÙthing else, creôÙates more ãvisuôÙal runôÙways for your eyes.ã
On the horôÙiôÙzonôÙtal plane, few misôÙtakes could be as wideôÙly comôÙmitôÙted as pushôÙing a sofa up against the wall. ProôÙfesôÙsionôÙal designôÙers preôÙfer to ãfloatã their furôÙniôÙture, leavôÙing ãa gap that hints at hidôÙden depth.ã To betôÙter underôÙstand this pheôÙnomôÙeôÙnon, conôÙsidôÙer how landôÙscape painters tend clearôÙly to sepôÙaôÙrate the foreôÙground, the midôÙdle ground, and the backôÙground: with the midôÙdle ground of the sofa flush against the backôÙground of the wall, ãthe brain learns to read them as a sinôÙgle flat plane.ã SepôÙaôÙraôÙtion introôÙduces definôÙing shadôÙows, a mediôÙum that can yield much greater results if manipôÙuôÙlatôÙed with lamps and othôÙer forms of direcôÙtionôÙal lightôÙing, as opposed to overôÙhead fixôÙtures that flood the space with uniôÙform light. GivôÙen the near-uniôÙverôÙsalôÙiôÙty of against-the-wall sofas and fluôÙoôÙresôÙcent lightôÙing cranked up to the max in Seoul, where I live, a KoreôÙan verôÙsion of this video couldôÙnãt come out too soon.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Frank Gehry Designed His Own Home, and What It TeachôÙes About CreôÙative Risk
NevôÙer Too Small: ArchiôÙtects Give Tours of Tiny Homes in Paris, MelôÙbourne, Milan, Hong Kong & Beyond
VisôÙit the Homes That Great ArchiôÙtects Designed for ThemôÙselves: Frank Lloyd Wright, Le CorôÙbusier, WalôÙter Gropius & Frank Gehry
The Tiny TransôÙformôÙing ApartôÙment: 8 Rooms in 420 Square Feet
Edgar Allan Poe Offers InteôÙriôÙor Design Advice and Blasts AmerôÙiôÙcan ArisôÙtoôÙcrats in ãThe PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of FurôÙniôÙtureã (1840)
After a Tour of Slavoj é§iéƒekãs Pad, Youãll NevôÙer See InteôÙriôÙor Design in the Same Way
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Why Salvador DalûÙ and Luis BuûÝuel Made the Still-Shocking Un Chien Andalou (1929)
description:
in Film, History |   	April 30th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Under most cirôÙcumôÙstances, thereãs nothôÙing parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly shockôÙing about cutôÙ
body:
in Film, History |   	April 30th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Under most cirôÙcumôÙstances, thereãs nothôÙing parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly shockôÙing about cutôÙting into an eye removed from a dead aniôÙmal. GraôÙtuôÙitous, maybe, and sureôÙly disôÙgustôÙing for some, but cerôÙtainôÙly not psyôÙchoôÙlogôÙiôÙcalôÙly damôÙagôÙing. I rememôÙber a man turnôÙing up one day to my first-grade classôÙroom and showôÙing us how to disôÙsect a real sheepôÙãs eye, which most of us found a fasôÙciôÙnatôÙing break from our usuôÙal spelling and math exerôÙcisôÙes. But in eduôÙcaôÙtion as in art, conôÙtext is everyôÙthing, and it is the conôÙtext estabôÙlished by SalôÙvador DalûÙ and Luis BuûÝuel that has allowed their own act of eye-slicôÙing to retain its visôÙcerôÙal impact. It occurs, of course, in their short film Un Chien Andalou, from 1929, the subôÙject of the new NerdôÙwriter video above.
The shot of BuûÝuelãs hand takôÙing a razor to the disôÙemôÙbodôÙied eye of what he latôÙer said was a calf comes earôÙly in the picôÙture. What gives it its powôÙer are the images that preôÙcede it: BuûÝuel sharpôÙenôÙing a razor and gazôÙing up at the moon, and the actress Simone Mareuil havôÙing her own eye opened up and the razor brought near. In extreme close-up, the calfãs eye obviôÙousôÙly isnãt Mareuilãs, but no matôÙter.
CinôÙeôÙma is so often about carôÙryôÙing the audiôÙence along with sheer momenôÙtum, and in any case, Un Chien Andalou is a work of surôÙreôÙalôÙism. To the extent that any comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of shots makes sense, it fails on that moveôÙmenôÙtãs terms. DalûÙ and BuûÝuel sucôÙceedôÙed, posôÙsiôÙbly to a unique degree, in makôÙing a film in which nothôÙing adds up. ãThe rule was to refuse any image that could have a ratioôÙnal meanôÙing, or any memôÙoôÙry or culôÙture,ã says BuûÝuel in a late interôÙview clip includôÙed in the video.
NerdôÙwriter creôÙator Evan Puschak lists a few of the images that made the cut: ãA crowd surôÙroundôÙing a man pokôÙing a sevôÙered hand with a stick; a man dragôÙging two Jesuit priests, one played by DalûÙ himôÙself, as well as two pianos laden with two decomôÙposôÙing, oozôÙing donôÙkeys; a womôÙanãs armpit hair sudôÙdenôÙly appearôÙing over a manãs vanôÙished mouth.ã The goal of assemôÙbling such grotesôÙqueries into one disôÙorôÙdered viewôÙing expeôÙriôÙence? ãBuûÝuel felt that mainôÙstream cinôÙeôÙma, so conôÙcerned with re-creôÙatôÙing the conôÙvenôÙtions of the nineôÙteenth-cenôÙtuôÙry novôÙel, was trapôÙping itself in the same insidôÙiôÙous moralôÙiôÙty and limôÙitôÙing its creôÙative potenôÙtial. He and DalûÙ sought to libôÙerôÙate the mediôÙum and the audiôÙence, and that libôÙerôÙaôÙtion was not designed to be pleasôÙant.ã NearôÙly a cenôÙtuôÙry on, Un Chien Andalouô remains memôÙoôÙrably trouôÙbling, but most of cinôÙeôÙma still stubôÙbornôÙly refusôÙes to be freed.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The Short SurôÙreôÙalôÙist Film That RevôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙized CinôÙeôÙma: Luis BuûÝuel & SalôÙvador DalûÙãs Un Chien Andalou (1929)

title: The Productive Writing Routines of Haruki Murakami, Stephen King, and Virginia Woolf, Explained
description:
in Literature, Writing |   	April 29th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Just days ago, HaruôÙki Murakamiãs JapanôÙese pubôÙlishôÙer announced that his sixôÙt
body:
in Literature, Writing |   	April 29th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Just days ago, HaruôÙki Murakamiãs JapanôÙese pubôÙlishôÙer announced that his sixôÙteenth novôÙel will come out this sumôÙmer. A brief secôÙtion of The Tale of KAHO, transôÙlatôÙed into EngôÙlish by Philip Gabriel, appeared in the New YorkôÙer in 2024. The full book will run to 352 pages, makôÙing it a fairôÙly hefty work for a 77-year-old novôÙelôÙist whoãs been at it for almost half a cenôÙtuôÙry now. Murakamiãs unflagôÙging proôÙducôÙtivôÙiôÙty must owe someôÙthing to his famousôÙly rigôÙorôÙousô conôÙstrucôÙtion of his life around the twin poles of writôÙing and runôÙning, two activôÙiôÙties that demand long-term endurance. In this video, the YouTuôÙber MariôÙWritôÙing attempts it herôÙself: wakôÙing up every mornôÙing at 4:00 a.m., workôÙing on a sinôÙgle project for five to six hours, then runôÙning ten kiloôÙmeôÙters ã or, in her case, at least getôÙting out and walkôÙing for a while.
HowôÙevôÙer indisôÙpensôÙable MurakaôÙmi may conôÙsidôÙer runôÙning to his writôÙing life, heãs also employed othôÙer idioôÙsynôÙcratôÙic and seemôÙingôÙly effecôÙtive techôÙniques of which othôÙers can make use. Take, for examôÙple, the way he got over the block stopôÙping him from makôÙing progress on his first novôÙel by writôÙing its openôÙing chapôÙter in EngôÙlish, then transôÙlatôÙing it back into his native JapanôÙese.
He also adheres to an editôÙing process conôÙsistôÙing of four spaced-out phasôÙes, each one focused on a difôÙferôÙent eleôÙment of the manôÙuôÙscript. Things work a bit difôÙferôÙentôÙly for Stephen King, whoãs less than two years oldôÙer than MurakaôÙmi, but has pubôÙlished 67 novôÙels, twelve stoôÙry colôÙlecôÙtions, and five books of nonôÙficôÙtion, among many othôÙer projects. Yet, as underôÙscored in MariôÙWritôÙingãs video here, King, no less than MurakaôÙmi, writes in a wholôÙly rouôÙtinized way that conôÙstiôÙtutes ãself-hypôÙnoôÙsis.ã
VirôÙginia Woolf probôÙaôÙbly got herôÙself into a simôÙiôÙlar state now and again, but givôÙen that she worked on a weekôÙly deadôÙline as a book critôÙic for some three decades, she no doubt had many occaôÙsions when she just had to put pen to paper no matôÙter what the state of her mind. And put pen to paper she litôÙerôÙalôÙly did: as MariôÙWritôÙing explains in this final video, Woolf wrote first inô longôÙhand (someôÙtimes in ink of her favorite colôÙor, purôÙple), then retyped the mornôÙingãs work after lunch. In addiôÙtion to her ficôÙtion and litôÙerôÙary jourôÙnalôÙism, she also made a post-tea daiôÙly habit of writôÙing more freely in her diary, which let her work out her thinkôÙing about her ãrealã projects. We might comôÙpare the imporôÙtance of Woolfãs diary to that of David Sedarisã diary, the founôÙdaôÙtion of everyôÙthing heãs pubôÙlished. But whether man or woman, EastôÙernôÙer or WestôÙernôÙer, novôÙelôÙist or othôÙerôÙwise, we writôÙers can all take from Woolfãs examôÙple the necesôÙsiôÙty of a dedôÙiôÙcatôÙed space: a room, that is, of oneãs own.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The DaiôÙly RouôÙtines of Famous CreôÙative PeoôÙple, PreôÙsentôÙed in an InterôÙacôÙtive InfoôÙgraphôÙic
HaruôÙki Murakamiãs DaiôÙly RouôÙtine: Up at 4:00 a.m., 5ã6 Hours of WritôÙing, Then a 10K Run
Stephen Kingãs 20 Rules for WritôÙers
David Sedaris Breaks Down His WritôÙing Process: Keep a Diary, CarôÙry a NoteôÙbook, Read Out Loud, AbanôÙdon Hope
Write Only 500 Words Per Day and PubôÙlish 50+ Books: GraôÙham Greeneãs WritôÙing Method
The DaiôÙly Habits of Famous WritôÙers: Franz KafôÙka, HaruôÙki MurakaôÙmi, Stephen King & More
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Simpsons Present Edgar Allan Poeãs ãThe Raven,ã and Teachers Now Use It to Teach Kids the Joys of Literature
description:
in Animation, Literature, Television |   	April 30th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

The SimpôÙsons has mocked or refôÙerôÙenced litôÙerôÙaôÙture over its many
body:
in Animation, Literature, Television |   	April 30th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

The SimpôÙsons has mocked or refôÙerôÙenced litôÙerôÙaôÙture over its many seaôÙsons, usuôÙalôÙly through a book Lisa was readôÙing, or with guest appearôÙances (e.g., Michael Chabon & Jonathan Franzen, Maya Angelou and Amy Tan). And it has refôÙerôÙenced Edgar Allan Poe in both title (ãThe Tell-Tale Headã from the first seaôÙson) and in passôÙing (in ãLisaãs Rivalã from 1994, the title charôÙacôÙter builds a dioôÙraôÙma based on the same Poe tale.)
But on the first ever ãTreeôÙhouse of HorôÙrorã from 1990ãthe SimpôÙsonsã recurôÙring HalôÙloween episodeãthey adaptôÙed Poeãs ãThe Ravenã more faithôÙfulôÙly than any bit of lit found in any othôÙer episode. The poem, read by James Earl Jones, remains intact, more or less, but with Dan Castellanetaãs Homer SimpôÙson proôÙvidôÙing the unnamed narratorãs voice. Marge makes an appearôÙance as the long departôÙed Lenore, withô hair so tall it needs an extra canôÙvas to conôÙtain it in porôÙtrait. MagôÙgie and Lisa are the censer-swingôÙing seraphim, and Bart is the annoyôÙing raven that driôÙves Homer insane.
CastelôÙlanôÙeôÙta does a great job delivôÙerôÙing Poeãs verse with conôÙvicôÙtion and humor, while keepôÙing the charôÙacôÙter true to both Homer and Poe. Itãs a balôÙancôÙing act hardôÙer than it sounds.
SufôÙfice it to say that this forôÙay into Poe was good enough for sevôÙerôÙal teachôÙersã guides (includôÙing this one from The New York Times) to sugôÙgest using the video in class. (Weãd love to hear about this if you were a teacher or stuôÙdent who expeôÙriôÙenced this.) And itãs the first and only time that Poe got co-writôÙing credôÙit on a SimpôÙsons episode.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The SimpôÙsons Pay WonôÙderôÙful TribôÙute to the AniôÙme of Hayao MiyazaôÙki
Watch The SimpôÙsonsã HalôÙloween ParôÙoôÙdy of Kubrickãs A ClockôÙwork Orange and The ShinôÙing
Thomas PynôÙchon Edits His Lines on The SimpôÙsons: ãHomer is my role modôÙel and I canãt speak ill of him.ã
 Ted Mills is a freeôÙlance writer on the arts.

title: Laura K. Sayers' Vibrant Postage Stamps Celebrate the Beauty of Everyday Moments
description:
All images courtesy of the artist, shared with permission
April 30, 2026
ArtCraftIllustration
Kate Mothes

From recognizable scenes around her home i
body:
All images courtesy of the artist, shared with permission
April 30, 2026
ArtCraftIllustration
Kate Mothes

From recognizable scenes around her home in Scotland to delicately rendered snapshots of places she visits, Laura K. Sayersã meticulously crafted postage stamps nod to connections from afar. The artist, who also illustrates childrenãs books and is commissioned for special projects like greeting cards, incorporates itty-bitty cuts of colorful paper into tiny tableaux that can fit in the palm of a hand.
Much of the work seen here is currently on view solo in Sayersã solo exhibition of miniatures titled The Wee Small Hours at N. atelier. An array of everyday scenes is chronicled in a format we typically associate with significant events and remembrance, documenting fleeting moments like little treasures. Some of her recent pieces are inspired by the Finnish landscape that emerged during the artistãs stay at the Fiskars Artist-in-Residence program, organized by Onoma.

The Wee Small Hours continues through this weekend in Glasgow. And keep an eye out for a joint update from Tiny Art Show. Find more on Sayersã Instagram.









		Previous articleNext article

title: Lina Lapelytá Fills Hamburger Bahnhof with 400,000 Wood Blocks for Communal Building
description: Inside the cavernous former train station that now houses Hamburger Bahnhof, 400,000 wooden cubes stack and topple into piles. Conceived by Lithua
body:
Inside the cavernous former train station that now houses Hamburger Bahnhof, 400,000 wooden cubes stack and topple into piles. Conceived by Lithuanian artist Lina Lapelytá and commissioned by Chanel, ãWe Make Years Out of Hoursã is a large-scale installation that invites the public to remake structures from these 10-centimeter blocks made of pine and spruce.
Lapelytá often combines sound and performance and collaborates with both professionals and novices. This participatory work continues the artistãs interest in collective making and caretaking, particularly as it relates to shared authorship and how we might amend and reshape what currently exists.

A trio of weekly performances on Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturdays will feature a libretto with the words of 15 writers, including Vietnamese-American poet Ocean Vuong, Lebanese-American painter Etel Adnan, Iranian filmmaker Forugh Farrokhzad, and Palestinian poet Mahmoud Darwish. Centered around community, love, and loss, these songs create another dimension in the space to consider agency and hope.
ãWe Make Years Out of Hoursã opens on May 1 and is on view through January 10, 2027, in Berlin. Explore more of Lapelytáãs multi-disciplinary works on her website and Instagram.






Portrait of Lina Lapelytá

schema.headline: Lina Lapelytá Fills Hamburger Bahnhof with 400,000 Wood Blocks for Communal Building

title: Dozens of Suspended 'Halos' Glimmer in a Florentine Factory
description: Earlier this month, dozens of metallic discs suspended from the ceiling of a large industrial space invited viewers to immerse themselves in what
body:
Earlier this month, dozens of metallic discs suspended from the ceiling of a large industrial space invited viewers to immerse themselves in what SpY describes as ãa continuous choreography of movement and reflection.ã The artist is known for his large-scale installations, often repurposing objects like traffic cones and metallic rescue blankets to create striking urban interventions.
SpYãs most recent room-scale work, titled ãHalos,ã reimagined the industrial interior of a former railway-related factory in Florenceãa place we typically associate with Renaissance elegance as opposed to brutalist designãas part of the cityãs Bright Festival.

Three stories high, ãHalosã interacts with the natural breeze that flows throughout the space, which is exacerbated by people moving around. Glimmering light further lends a sense of ethereality and even magic. 
See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Dozens of Suspended 'Halos' Glimmer in a Florentine Factory
